episode_title: Fabian Heckenberg, Naja Nielsen & Gard Steiro: The Hard Truths About AI Every Newsroom Leader Can't Ignore

codes.description: Leadership in the AI era is characterized by acknowledging that nobody knows where things are going, fostering organizational discussion rather than dictating a single direction.
excerpts: my most important task as a leader is not to point direction, but to foster a discussion within the organization
there are a lot of disagreements in VG about the future and AI. And I really think that is that's a tool for me
codes.speaker: Gard Steiro

codes.description: Using multiple scenario frameworks instead of fixed strategies to navigate AI uncertainty, experimenting in different directions simultaneously.
excerpts: we work with scenarios. So we have different scenarios for how this can develop for the next years and we try to say what can we do to experiment and move in other try to to move in different directions
if you looked at the latest shift, we know that we had to move from A to B. No, we don't really know what which direction things are moving
codes.speaker: Gard Steiro

codes.description: Deliberate organizational choice to focus on opportunities rather than threats from AI, framed as a survival strategy.
excerpts: you can focus on threats all day, but if we focus on the possibilities, there is a chance that we can. Well, foster and survive
codes.speaker: Gard Steiro

codes.description: Critique of the legacy assumption that the editor-in-chief must be the smartest person in the room and make all important decisions, identified as the worst cultural inheritance in news organizations.
excerpts: the worst legacy we've got in media Especially in news organization, is that we believe the one that is the editor in chief must be the most clever man in the room and therefore uh he or she must make all decisions of importance
codes.speaker: Naja Nielsen

codes.description: In newsrooms, strategic decisions are accidentally delegated to the floor while leaders waste time on minutiae that could be tested, inverting the proper decision hierarchy.
excerpts: what you see often is that actually strategic decisions are by accident delegated to the floor. People don't know whether to go in one or the other direction, whereas kind of very minutiae detailed discussion, should it be orange or should it be purple? Something you shouldn't discuss if you can test it.
codes.speaker: Naja Nielsen

codes.description: Call to adopt Silicon Valley working methods (Amazon, Netflix-style rapid iteration) despite moral reservations about tech companies, because newsrooms failed to do so in previous disruption cycles.
excerpts: we really need in this round to learn from Silicon Valley... we need to copy their way of working and remember that Amazon, Netflix And we need to do this in this round because we didn't do it in the first
codes.speaker: Naja Nielsen

codes.description: Creating organizational guidelines that acknowledge both what is known and what remains unknown about AI, while carving out space for experimentation.
excerpts: It's very important to create kind of a transformative framework. To give the newsroom and the organization some guidelines and to point out what is going on, what is happening, but also on the other hand what we don't know about
codes.speaker: Fabian Heckenberger

codes.description: AI projects require bringing together editorial, product, tech, and business people who jointly own problems, rather than assigning ownership to a single department.
excerpts: the most important thing that we bring People from different parts of the negotiation together and that they own the problems and they try to find the solutions
The problem in many newsrooms is that these different parts of the organization they don't really understand each other
codes.speaker: Gard Steiro

codes.description: Newsrooms have become more comfortable working with product teams but remain resistant to integrating business strategy into editorial AI projects, creating a dangerous gap.
excerpts: I think there is a lack of business development in the newsroom now... newsroom is not so reluctant to work with product, but they are very reluctant when I say Let's work on the business strategy with this project as well
If we don't in the AI shift, think about new business models for journalism, then the products can be as good as we hope for, but we will will not be able to earn enough money to finance journalism
codes.speaker: Gard Steiro

codes.description: Unresolved tension between building features for current subscribers, testing Gen AI features, and developing new tools, with no clear framework for resolving competing demands.
excerpts: Do we implement, do we build this product feature with SERS, our subscribers right now? Do we have to test These new Gen AI features? Do we have to develop the new tools? And this is kind of a prioritization battle. And we haven't really figured out how to navigate through this battle.
codes.speaker: Fabian Heckenberger

codes.description: Setting strict one-year deadlines for AI projects with clear problem definitions and measurable goals, shutting down projects that fail to show progress.
excerpts: I said this is the problem you're gonna solve, visa goals, and we give you one year. And if you don't have any results at one year, then we'll shut you down
codes.speaker: Gard Steiro

codes.description: Selection bias in user testing where new AI products are evaluated by loyal super users who resist change, producing misleading negative feedback.
excerpts: we tested the new product on our super users. And they did really hated it because they are used to seeing news the way we present them
if you want to test new products for new users. You need to recruit users that you don't have and test on them
codes.speaker: Gard Steiro

codes.description: Research finding that younger audiences rank relevance as the primary driver of trust in news brands, more important than traditional markers like transparency.
excerpts: why do you trust a news brand? And what came up on list was relevance. It really has to be relevant for you. And I think that that is what we are not good enough at all
codes.speaker: Gard Steiro

codes.description: Audience distrust of news media stems not from doubting journalists' competence but from questioning whether organizations genuinely prioritize audience interests.
excerpts: People are not in doubt that we are competent. They are in doubt about whether we want to do them well. Whether we really have their interests at our hearts and also whether our heart is in the right place because they see so much bullshit around.
codes.speaker: Naja Nielsen

codes.description: Recognition that blanket insistence on human-in-the-loop for all AI processes will prevent newsrooms from realizing AI's full potential, especially for personalization at scale.
excerpts: In the start of AI we insisted on humans in loop. And if we still insist on that, we won't take the advantages of AI It is impossible to have a you personalize our content with a human in loop
So we can't stay on that principle forever
codes.speaker: Gard Steiro

codes.description: Using one AI agent to perform a content task and a second agent to audit the output for errors, as a scalable alternative to human review.
excerpts: You have one algorithm, you want but doing one task And then you set up another agent that do another task in newsroom to try to find the mistakes. And so you have to work with your systems that you reduce the error uh the risk of making mistakes
codes.speaker: Gard Steiro

codes.description: AI errors contextualized against the reality that human journalists make comparable or identical mistakes, undermining the argument that AI is uniquely risky.
excerpts: we killed a Norwegian queen yesterday and that on our front page... And she is still alive. But we had it was a huge mistake... And that was a human
over the last twenty years this happened like a dozen times in our sports articles and there was no uh Gen AI involved
codes.speaker: Gard Steiro / Fabian Heckenberger

codes.description: The traditional article format is seen as dying or already dead for certain categories (especially daily news), while storytelling itself endures across new formats.
excerpts: I started in journalism 25 years ago and I still write my stories the exactly the same way. So the article will die, should die, storytelling won't die
news articles maybe already dead, maybe next year, and you have to think maybe in categories and in faces
codes.speaker: Gard Steiro / Fabian Heckenberger

codes.description: Risk that optimizing existing products for loyal users produces only incremental improvements insufficient to attract the next generation of news consumers.
excerpts: if we s work that way we will do incremental changes on the article format. We will do incremental changes our products today. And I'm not sure whether that will be enough or radical enough to cater for the next generation
if you look at Vici today, we have just moved the printed article into digital format and hoped it will work
codes.speaker: Gard Steiro

codes.description: Shift from thinking of audiences as passive consumers of broadcasts to active participants in news production and product development.
excerpts: the term is the audiences, right? And we are we're back to that thing where we want to be on the stage and you should be passive, the audiences, but that's not the job that we should uh that the audiences need to be solved for
not interdisciplinary, I think is including the public in the development
codes.speaker: Naja Nielsen

codes.description: Growing gap between older audiences' habitual news consumption and younger generations' entirely different relationship with information, requiring simultaneous service to both.
excerpts: the gap between their habits and the ways of access and information generationally will be bigger than ever before
The article will still exist, but it will be a very, very small subset of the population
codes.speaker: Naja Nielsen

codes.description: Current AI tools will be commoditized within a year; the strategic focus should be on identifying unique journalism that cannot be copied rather than on tooling.
excerpts: All the tools we are presenting here will be from the shelf in a year. So maybe it's maybe shouldn't focus so much on that and just wait. What we need to focus on is what really can be our unique selling point for the future
codes.speaker: Gard Steiro

codes.description: Framework for measuring AI ROI across three categories: subscriber retention, efficiency gains (hours saved), and pure experimentation, with the third basket being phased out.
excerpts: we think about kind of three baskets... We think about our subscriber business and we try to measure retention... We think about efficiency... and the third basket is testing and just playing with the AI for the sake of AI. But we try to move away this basket more and more because I think this phase should be over now
codes.speaker: Fabian Heckenberger

codes.description: Nordic trend toward foundation ownership of media companies as a potential model for financing quality journalism outside traditional market pressures.
excerpts: most media companies is now owned by foundations. And maybe that is part of the future that you need other ways of financing quality journalism
codes.speaker: Gard Steiro

codes.description: Public service broadcasters face political pressure to justify their funding if they primarily serve aging demographics and fail to reach younger citizens.
excerpts: there is no guarantee that public service is gonna continue in Europe. It is a live political debate everywhere
if we solve that for people that are sixties, seventy, eighties, no one else, then of course we shouldn't uh have the public support
codes.speaker: Naja Nielsen

codes.description: Paradoxical opportunity: as AI makes content creation easy and verification hard, there may be a three-year window where quality journalism becomes more valued.
excerpts: in a world where it's so easy to create content uh multimodal content, whatever, and it's getting harder and harder to verify if this this content is really true. Maybe there at least for the next maybe three years there's a there's a chance for really quality journalism to double down
codes.speaker: Fabian Heckenberger

codes.description: Counterintuitive argument that in some domains (surgery, flying, braking), humans already trust machines more than humans, and news may follow the same pattern.
excerpts: if I had to choose between a doctor doing the surgery and a machine right now, doing on my beating heart, I would prefer the machine
I don't think they only will be better, I think they will be more trusted
codes.speaker: Naja Nielsen

codes.description: Cultural blind spot where journalists are skilled at finding others' mistakes but poor at acknowledging their own, undermining the transparency needed for trust.
excerpts: journalists are really good at finding errors at other uh in other organizations but not so good at admitting them themselves. So this transparency is really important for trust
codes.speaker: Gard Steiro

codes.description: Certain journalism -- on-the-ground reporting, human curiosity, making stories relatable -- remains inherently human and cannot be automated.
excerpts: I need reporting. I need to meet people for whom it is important for me to relate to the story. I need ways into the story
for some events, for some things going on in this world, for some political developments, you need humans. Seeing it, thinking about it
codes.speaker: Naja Nielsen / Fabian Heckenberger

codes.description: Nordic organizational culture of openness, transparency, and willingness to discuss uncertainty gives Scandinavian media a structural advantage in navigating AI transitions.
excerpts: the openness in this the Scandinavian market is one of the reasons... we have that discussions. You are open about uncertainty And when you open a button certainty for the organization, then there are possibility to create like solutions
codes.speaker: Gard Steiro

codes.description: Concern that the advertising revenue pillar may collapse for smaller players, leaving the two-pillar business model (ads plus subscriptions) unsustainable.
excerpts: I'm concerned about the ad market. I think it will still be revenue there for big players in the market, for smaller players, I'm not sure
we have to look into new revenue streams, maybe small revenue streams, not looking for the unicorn
codes.speaker: Gard Steiro

episode_summary: This live panel at the Nordic AI in Media Summit surfaces a striking leadership consensus: nobody knows where AI is taking journalism, and the honest acknowledgment of that uncertainty is itself a strategic asset. Gard Steiro (VG) advocates scenario planning over fixed roadmaps and time-boxed experimentation with clear kill criteria, while Naja Nielsen (SVT) challenges the editor-in-chief as oracle model and argues that audience distrust stems from perceived indifference rather than incompetence. The panelists converge on a provocation that the article format is dying (or should be), that human-in-the-loop must be selectively abandoned for AI personalization to work at scale, and that newsrooms dangerously avoid integrating business strategy into editorial AI projects. Beneath the pragmatic Nordic composure lies genuine anxiety about unsolved problems: no new business model has emerged, the prioritization battle between current subscribers and future audiences remains unresolved, and public service broadcasters face existential political pressure to prove relevance to younger citizens.
surprises: Gard Steiro explicitly says VG should move beyond human-in-the-loop, a rare public admission from a major editor-in-chief that editorial oversight cannot scale to AI personalization.
Naja Nielsen argues machines may be MORE trusted than humans for certain tasks, inverting the typical newsroom assumption that human review equals quality.
The panel acknowledges that killing a Norwegian queen on the front page (a human error) illustrates that human editorial processes are also unreliable.
Fabian Heckenberger suggests news articles may already be dead and proposes a phased approach to sunsetting different article types.
Steiro confesses the business model question keeps him awake every night and that he genuinely does not have the answer.
tensions: Between serving loyal existing users who hate change and building radical new products for future audiences who do not yet exist.
Between insisting on human-in-the-loop for editorial quality and needing to abandon it for AI personalization at scale.
Between acknowledging tools will be commoditized in a year and still needing to invest heavily in current AI experimentation.
Between editorial independence and the need to integrate business strategy into every AI project.
Between Nordic openness as a cultural strength and the global competitive threat from Silicon Valley companies that move faster.
Between public service broadcaster mandates to serve all citizens and the practical reality of primarily reaching older demographics.
